era_context: The early 18th century in Europe is marked by the War of Spanish Succession (1701–1714), a major dynastic conflict reshaping European power. The Ottoman Empire remains a significant regional power in the Mediterranean and Eastern Europe. In Asia, the Qing Dynasty under the Kangxi Emperor is consolidating control over China and expanding into Central Asia. Japan is in the Edo period under the Tokugawa shogunate. The Age of Exploration has given way to colonial consolidation, with European powers competing for dominance in the Americas, Africa, and Asia.

events.title: War of Spanish Succession begins
events.region: Europe (Spain, France, Holy Roman Empire, England, Dutch Republic)
events.description: Following the death of Charles II of Spain without direct heir, Louis XIV of France supports his grandson Philip of Anjou's claim to the Spanish throne. England, the Dutch Republic, and the Holy Roman Empire oppose French expansion and form the Grand Alliance to contest the succession. This conflict will dominate European politics for over a decade, reshaping territorial boundaries and establishing Britain as a major naval and colonial power. The war involves campaigns across Europe, the Mediterranean, and colonial territories in North America and the Caribbean.
key_figures: Philip of Anjou (claimant to Spanish throne, later Philip V)
William III (King of England and Dutch stadtholder)

sources.name: Contemporary diplomatic correspondence and court records from Versailles, Vienna, and London
Memoirs of the Duke of Saint-Simon
Historiographic consensus on War of Spanish Succession

events.certainty_note: The outbreak of the War of Spanish Succession in 1701 is one of the most thoroughly documented events of the early modern period, with abundant contemporary diplomatic records and later historical accounts.

events.title: Frederick I crowned King of Prussia
events.description: Frederick III, Elector of Brandenburg, crowns himself Frederick I, King in Prussia, in Königsberg. This act elevates Brandenburg-Prussia to kingdom status and reflects the growing power and ambitions of the Hohenzollern dynasty. The coronation is performed without papal approval, asserting Prussian independence. This marks a significant step in Prussia's rise as a major European power, though the kingdom remains relatively modest in size and influence compared to France, Austria, and Russia at this time.
key_figures: Frederick I (Elector of Brandenburg, newly crowned King in Prussia)

sources.name: Contemporary Prussian court records and coronation documents

events.certainty_note: The coronation of Frederick I is well-documented in Prussian archives and contemporary European diplomatic records.

events.title: Yale College founded
events.description: Yale College is established in Killingworth (now Clinton), Connecticut, as the Collegiate School, founded by clergy to train Protestant ministers. The institution reflects the educational priorities of Puritan New England and will become one of the most prestigious universities in North America. The school is initially modest in scale but will grow to become a major center of learning and influence in the American colonies and later the United States.

sources.name: Yale College founding records and charter

events.certainty_note: Yale's founding in 1701 is well-documented in institutional records and colonial American historical sources.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor's Southern Tour (sixth tour)
events.description: The Kangxi Emperor undertakes his sixth and final major tour of southern China, traveling through the Yangtze River valley and coastal provinces. These tours serve multiple purposes: consolidating imperial authority, inspecting public works and water management systems, and gathering intelligence on regional conditions. The tours are carefully documented and serve as propaganda for imperial benevolence and competence. This particular tour reinforces Qing control over the prosperous southern provinces and demonstrates the emperor's commitment to good governance.

sources.name: Kangxi Emperor's travel records and official court documents

events.certainty_note: The Kangxi Emperor's southern tours are extensively documented in Qing imperial records and contemporary accounts.

events.title: Forty-Seven Ronin incident (Ako incident)
events.description: Following the forced suicide (seppuku) of their lord Asano Naganori in 1701 due to an incident at the Shogun's court, forty-seven masterless samurai (ronin) plot revenge against Kira Yoshinaka, the official responsible for their lord's disgrace. Though the actual revenge attack occurs in December 1702, the incident begins in 1701 with Asano's death and the ronin's decision to seek vengeance. This event becomes one of the most celebrated stories in Japanese culture, embodying the samurai code of loyalty (bushido) and honor. The incident is extensively documented in contemporary records and later becomes the subject of numerous literary and theatrical works.

sources.name: Edo period court records and shogunal documents
Chushingura (The Treasury of Loyal Retainers) - later literary compilation

events.certainty_note: Asano Naganori's death in 1701 is confirmed by contemporary Edo period records. The subsequent revenge plot and attack are well-documented historical events, though later literary versions embellish details.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the War of Spanish Succession's outbreak is sometimes overstated in Western historiography as a purely European affair; however, modern scholarship emphasizes its global dimensions and impact on colonial competition, particularly in North America and the Caribbean. The founding of Yale College, while culturally significant for American intellectual history, was not immediately recognized as such in 1701; its prominence is partly a retrospective assessment. The Forty-Seven Ronin incident, while celebrated in Japanese culture, was initially viewed by the Tokugawa shogunate as a serious breach of order requiring severe punishment, not as a heroic act—the romanticization is a later cultural development.
historiographic_note: The year 1701 is exceptionally well-documented for Europe and East Asia, with abundant primary sources from courts, diplomatic archives, and administrative records. Western historiography has traditionally emphasized the War of Spanish Succession at the expense of simultaneous developments in Asia. Japanese sources provide detailed accounts of the Asano incident, though these were later elaborated in literary form. Colonial American records are sparser but adequate for Yale's founding. The main bias is Eurocentric emphasis; non-European developments are often underrepresented in general histories despite being well-attested in regional sources.

graph_edges.note: War of Spanish Succession continues with major naval and land campaigns
Frederick I's elevation of Prussia contributes to its rise as a major power under Frederick the Great
Asano's death directly precipitates the ronin's revenge attack in 1702
